label: delphi-r1-p5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is UNLAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Starting from the strongest invalidity argument: the District's own admissions — that the $0.85 figure was derived only \"in part\" from the 1.6× marginal-cost study and in part from \"conservation-incentive pricing,\" and that the rebate program (55% of revenue) \"mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge\" — track almost exactly the defects condemned in Capistrano (rate divorced from actual marginal cost of service at the tier) plus an independent §6(b)(1)-(2) revenue-use/purpose problem (cross-subsidization to non-payers). Because the District bears the burden of proving §6(b) compliance by a preponderance of the evidence, and it has conceded facts undermining both the pricing methodology and the revenue-use nexus, a court is more likely than not to invalidate at least (a) the incentive-pricing increment above the 1.6× cost-justified level and (b) the rebate-funded 55% tranche as not proportional/not \"for\" the service the payors receive — either of which, let alone both, would be \"substantial\" under the proposition. Full District victory is possible only if a court applies Coziahr/Wilde-style deference to a blended cost-plus-conservation methodology and accepts that turf/appliance rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs enough to benefit payors too, but the District's own concessions make that a harder sell than in the cases where agencies prevailed.","rationales":["Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 requires that tiered/surcharge water rates be tied to the actual marginal cost of service at that tier and holds conservation policy alone cannot justify pricing above cost; the District's admission that $0.85 rests 'in part' on 'conservation-incentive pricing' rather than solely the 1.6x cost study is close to a per se Capistrano violation for the excess increment.","Article XIII D, section 6(b)(1)-(2) bars revenues from exceeding the funds required to provide the service and bars using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which it was imposed; funneling 55% of surcharge revenue into rebates that the District concedes 'mostly benefit' non-paying customers is a direct, admitted mismatch between who pays and who benefits, independently vulnerable under this prong regardless of how the pricing issue comes out.","Under Prop. 218/Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105, the agency bears the burden of proving compliance with section 6(b) by a preponderance of the evidence in a validation-type proceeding, so ambiguous or concededly-mixed cost/incentive rate-setting shifts the practical risk of loss onto the District rather than the challengers.","Countervailing authority such as Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850 shows courts will sometimes defer to reasonable cost-of-service methodologies (e.g., base-extra-capacity, peaking-cost allocation) even without engineering precision, and the District's rate study does identify a real 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that could support a materially similar surcharge if the incentive-pricing gloss is stripped out, leaving room for partial rather than total invalidation or even survival if the gap between $0.85 and the cost-justified amount proves small.","The 30% debt-service allocation for the recycled-water plant is plausibly defensible as a genuine cost of providing water supply/capacity to high-tier users (arguably within 6(b)'s 'cost of service'), which tends to insulate that slice from invalidation and supports a 'substantial part' rather than 'whole' invalidation outcome, consistent with how the proposition is framed."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105","Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Cal. Water Code § 375","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop. 26 tax/fee distinction)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the actual numeric gap between the $0.85 rate and the amount strictly justified by the 1.6× marginal-cost differential is large (favoring invalidation, as in Capistrano) or small/negligible (favoring a Coziahr-style deference finding that the rate is still reasonably cost-based despite the conservation-incentive framing) — the hypothetical doesn't quantify this gap, and it is likely outcome-determinative for whether a court invalidates the pricing component versus only the rebate cross-subsidy component."}

data.position_summary: Starting from the strongest invalidity argument: the District's own admissions — that the $0.85 figure was derived only "in part" from the 1.6× marginal-cost study and in part from "conservation-incentive pricing," and that the rebate program (55% of revenue) "mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge" — track almost exactly the defects condemned in Capistrano (rate divorced from actual marginal cost of service at the tier) plus an independent §6(b)(1)-(2) revenue-use/purpose problem (cross-subsidization to non-payers). Because the District bears the burden of proving §6(b) compliance by a preponderance of the evidence, and it has conceded facts undermining both the pricing methodology and the revenue-use nexus, a court is more likely than not to invalidate at least (a) the incentive-pricing increment above the 1.6× cost-justified level and (b) the rebate-funded 55% tranche as not proportional/not "for" the service the payors receive — either of which, let alone both, would be "substantial" under the proposition. Full District victory is possible only if a court applies Coziahr/Wilde-style deference to a blended cost-plus-conservation methodology and accepts that turf/appliance rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs enough to benefit payors too, but the District's own concessions make that a harder sell than in the cases where agencies prevailed.
rationales: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 requires that tiered/surcharge water rates be tied to the actual marginal cost of service at that tier and holds conservation policy alone cannot justify pricing above cost; the District's admission that $0.85 rests 'in part' on 'conservation-incentive pricing' rather than solely the 1.6x cost study is close to a per se Capistrano violation for the excess increment.

Article XIII D, section 6(b)(1)-(2) bars revenues from exceeding the funds required to provide the service and bars using fee revenue for any purpose other than that for which it was imposed; funneling 55% of surcharge revenue into rebates that the District concedes 'mostly benefit' non-paying customers is a direct, admitted mismatch between who pays and who benefits, independently vulnerable under this prong regardless of how the pricing issue comes out.

Under Prop. 218/Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105, the agency bears the burden of proving compliance with section 6(b) by a preponderance of the evidence in a validation-type proceeding, so ambiguous or concededly-mixed cost/incentive rate-setting shifts the practical risk of loss onto the District rather than the challengers.

Countervailing authority such as Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850 shows courts will sometimes defer to reasonable cost-of-service methodologies (e.g., base-extra-capacity, peaking-cost allocation) even without engineering precision, and the District's rate study does identify a real 1.6x marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that could support a materially similar surcharge if the incentive-pricing gloss is stripped out, leaving room for partial rather than total invalidation or even survival if the gap between $0.85 and the cost-justified amount proves small.

The 30% debt-service allocation for the recycled-water plant is plausibly defensible as a genuine cost of providing water supply/capacity to high-tier users (arguably within 6(b)'s 'cost of service'), which tends to insulate that slice from invalidation and supports a 'substantial part' rather than 'whole' invalidation outcome, consistent with how the proposition is framed.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105
Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop. 26 tax/fee distinction)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the actual numeric gap between the $0.85 rate and the amount strictly justified by the 1.6× marginal-cost differential is large (favoring invalidation, as in Capistrano) or small/negligible (favoring a Coziahr-style deference finding that the rate is still reasonably cost-based despite the conservation-incentive framing) — the hypothetical doesn't quantify this gap, and it is likely outcome-determinative for whether a court invalidates the pricing component versus only the rebate cross-subsidy component.
